title: Dog Regurgitating Undigested Food: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn why your dog is regurgitating undigested food, when to call the vet, and why photos of the incident help with a faster veterinary diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: Regurgitation is the passive expulsion of undigested food and is typically a medium-urgency concern unless accompanied by respiratory distress. Providing your vet with photos or videos of the incident can significantly speed up the diagnosis and treatment process.

What is regurgitation and how does it differ from vomiting?

Regurgitation is the passive expulsion of food or fluid from the esophagus before it reaches the stomach. Unlike vomiting, which is an active process involving abdominal heaving and nausea, regurgitation often happens suddenly and without warning. The food typically appears undigested and may even be shaped like a tube (the shape of the esophagus).

Is my dog’s regurgitation considered a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for regurgitation is Medium.
  • While a single episode may be caused by eating too quickly, frequent or persistent regurgitation can lead to serious complications like aspiration pneumonia.
  • If your dog is also coughing, struggling to breathe, or acting lethargic, you should contact a veterinarian immediately.

How can taking a photo or video help my veterinarian triage my dog?

  • Documenting the incident with a photo or video is one of the most helpful things you can do for your vet.
  • A photo of the expelled material helps the clinical team distinguish between vomit and regurgitated food.
  • A video of the act itself can help identify the underlying cause, such as an esophageal disorder, leading to a faster and more accurate treatment plan.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

For acute vomiting, discontinue oral intake for at least 24 hours. Subsequently, introduce small, frequent meals of a bland, low-fat, easily digestible diet such as boiled lean beef, chicken, cottage cheese and rice, or a commercial prescription diet, gradually transitioning back to the usual diet over 3-5 days. Antiemetic drugs such as metoclopramide (0.3 mg/kg, PO or SC, tid, or 1-2 mg/kg/day CRI), maropitant (1 mg/kg/day, SC, or 2 mg/kg/day, PO, for 5 days), or ondansetron (0.1-1 mg/kg, PO, once to twice daily) should be considered after an etiologic diagnosis or if vomiting is protracted or severe, causing dehydration or electrolyte imbalances; note that metoclopramide is contraindicated in confirmed or suspected GI obstructions. For megaesophagus, the optimal diet consistency to prevent regurgitation varies; some dogs respond to soft gruel, while others do better with dry food or canned food formed into meatball shapes. Frequent, small meals are generally recommended. Feeding from an elevated position, with forelimbs higher than hindlimbs, followed by maintaining this position for 10-15 minutes after eating, can assist gravity in moving food into the stomach.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Emergency, Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 387)

faqs.question: How can I tell the difference between vomiting and regurgitation?
faqs.answer: Vomiting involves active stomach contractions and retching, whereas regurgitation is a passive, sudden movement of food out of the mouth without warning.

faqs.question: Should I feed my dog after they regurgitate?
faqs.answer: It is generally recommended to withhold food for a few hours and consult your veterinarian, as repeated episodes can increase the risk of the dog inhaling food into their lungs.

faqs.question: What are common causes of regurgitation?
faqs.answer: Common causes include eating too fast, esophageal blockages, or underlying medical conditions such as megaesophagus or acid reflux.
